title: WebNet: A biomateriomic three-dimensional spider web neural net
abstract: Spiders, silks and webs are abundant in most ecosystems, suggesting that they are a significant evolutionary success. The structures they build – in particular various forms of webs – have intrigued engineers for a long time, and elucidated inspiration for new mechanical designs of *de novo* materials. Here we report the development of a biomateriomic neural network based constitutive model to describe the mechanical features, such as strength and toughness, of a 3D spider web depending on salient structural features, specifically: average fiber lengths, fiber orientations, web connectivity, and web density. In particular, we focus our study on the structural and mechanical properties of a *Cyrtophora citricola* spider web, and report a method to derive the neural net model directly from the experimental–computational mesoscale modeling, using a novel data augmentation method. Our machine learning model captures the complex biomateriomical mechanics of spider webs. More generally, approaches such as reported here can be useful to describe the intricate relationships in other hierarchical materials, and provide a basis to develop multiscale models, and bridge experimental data with computational and theoretical modeling efforts.
results:
Using the data augmentation technique described in Section [2.2](#page-2-2), we build the dataset to train the neural network [\(Figs.](#page-4-1) [8](#page-4-1) and [9](#page-5-0), details in Sections [2.3](#page-5-1)[–2.5\)](#page-5-2). Once trained ([Fig.](#page-5-0) [9](#page-5-0)), the model enables us to make predictions about the web behavior as a function of its physical characteristics, defining the local microstructure.

First, we explore whether the model can accurately reproduce the mechanical signatures seen in the original, experimental derived data. [Fig.](#page-6-0) [10](#page-6-0) shows the predictions of WebNet, for a set of 100 random samples that were removed from the original complete dataset (described in Section [2.2\)](#page-2-2) to create a set of data that the model has not been trained or tested on, and could be used to test the model's performance. The predictions are conducted for strength in the *x, y*, and *z* -direction, as well as toughness in the *x, y*, and *z* -direction. For all cases, the test (red curve) and prediction (blue) agree remarkably well. We note that the model is capable of discerning detailed variations of the relationships. These results show that the model has adequately learned the mechanical signatures of the web geometries as a function of microstructure.

Next, we explore the predictions of strength and toughness values as a function of key physical parameters, namely average connectivity, density, and fiber orientation. [Fig.](#page-7-0) [11](#page-7-0) depicts predictions from the WebNet model of strength and toughness values, for variation of average connectivity with boundaries (A), density (B), and orientation along the *x*-axis (C). These predictions reveal that the model captures the general trends of the variations of parameters well. For example, increases in density lead to increases in strength and toughness.
conclusion:
This study on the structural and mechanical properties of a *C. citricola* spider web reported a method to derive the neural net model directly from the experimental–computational mesoscale modeling, using a novel data augmentation method. The data augmentation method can be used in other application where sparse experimental or computational data is available, to build larger datasets needed for machine learning applications. This is often a key aspect of data driven models, and could be transferred to other systems.

Our results show that our machine learning model captures the complex mechanics of spider webs, and hence provides a rapid and insightful tool to predict the mechanical signatures of webs. More generally, such approaches can be useful to describe the intricate relationships in other hierarchical materials, and provide a basis to develop multiscale models. We believe that modeling approach reported here can provide fruitful approaches at the interface of experimental and computational data, integrating experimental mechanics into a predictive design tools. Indeed, an application of the model can be materials-by-design, where the ML model can be used to solve an inverse problem to identify certain material microstructures to meet certain target material combinations [\[48\]](#page-9-13).

Future work could focus on further validation of the method, perhaps by comparing the predictions to other, distinct spider webs (from the same species and others). This may provide a more independent validation of the results and offer new insights into general understanding of web mechanics, and our ability to model the relationships between microstructure and mechanical features using machine learning.
notes: Used 'Results' section as 'results' and left 'discussion' empty as no separate discussion section exists.
